source_of_truth.title: SEO BIBLE 2026
source_of_truth.policy: Part 10 primary-source corrections override conflicting earlier sections. Evidence labels must never be promoted without new evidence.

claims.claim: Google supports user-agent, allow, disallow, and sitemap fields in robots.txt.
claims.public_wording: Google documents four supported robots.txt fields: user-agent, allow, disallow, and sitemap.
claims.prohibited_wording: Google supports arbitrary robots.txt directives.

claims.claim: Google does not support crawl-delay in robots.txt.
claims.public_wording: Google does not support crawl-delay in robots.txt.
claims.prohibited_wording: Set Googlebot crawl speed with crawl-delay.

claims.claim: Google processes up to 500 KiB of robots.txt and ignores content after that ceiling.
claims.public_wording: Google documents a 500 KiB robots.txt processing limit.
claims.prohibited_wording: Google processes an unlimited robots.txt file.

claims.claim: robots.txt rules apply only to the host, protocol, and port where the file is served.
claims.public_wording: Each relevant host, protocol, and port needs its own applicable robots.txt behavior.
claims.prohibited_wording: The main domain robots.txt automatically governs every subdomain and asset host.

claims.claim: Google uses the longest matching robots.txt path; on equal specificity, the least restrictive rule wins.
claims.public_wording: Google resolves robots.txt conflicts by longest match and least-restrictive ties.
claims.prohibited_wording: Allow always overrides Disallow.

claims.claim: Google treats robots.txt 4xx responses other than 429 as if no crawl restrictions exist.
claims.public_wording: A missing robots.txt returning 404 is not a crawl block for Google.
claims.prohibited_wording: A missing robots.txt blocks Google.

claims.claim: On robots.txt 5xx, 429, or network errors, Google initially pauses crawling, then can use a last-known-good cached file before eventually treating the site as having no robots restrictions if the host remains available.
claims.public_wording: A robots.txt server error can temporarily interrupt crawling and trigger cached-rule behavior.
claims.prohibited_wording: Any robots.txt error permanently stops all crawling.

claims.claim: A robots.txt disallow prevents content crawling but does not guarantee URL removal from search results.
claims.public_wording: Disallow controls crawling, not guaranteed deindexing.
claims.prohibited_wording: Use robots.txt to remove a URL from Google.

claims.claim: An HTTP 2xx response makes content eligible for processing but does not guarantee indexing.
claims.public_wording: A 200 response is necessary evidence for a normal indexable page, not proof of indexing.
claims.prohibited_wording: A 200 page is indexed.

claims.claim: Google documents 301 and 308 as strong redirect signals and 302 and 307 as weaker signals.
claims.public_wording: Use permanent redirects for permanent moves and temporary redirects for temporary moves.
claims.prohibited_wording: All redirect status codes are identical.

claims.claim: Google treats 404 and 410 similarly for indexing removal.
claims.public_wording: Google removes previously indexed URLs that return either 404 or 410.
claims.prohibited_wording: 410 always deindexes materially faster than 404.

claims.claim: Google treats HTTP 429 as a server-overload signal.
claims.public_wording: A 429 response signals overload and may reduce crawling.
claims.prohibited_wording: 429 behaves like an ordinary missing page.

claims.claim: A soft 404 is a successful HTTP response whose content communicates missing or empty content.
claims.public_wording: Return a real missing-page status for genuinely missing content instead of a 200 error page.
claims.prohibited_wording: A visually empty 200 page is technically healthy.

claims.claim: Google documents nosnippet as preventing page content from being used as direct input for AI Overviews and AI Mode, while also suppressing normal snippets.
claims.public_wording: nosnippet restricts normal snippets and direct use in Google AI features; it is a significant visibility trade-off.
claims.prohibited_wording: nosnippet blocks only AI Overviews without affecting search snippets.

claims.claim: Google documents max-snippet as limiting snippet length and how much content may be used as direct input for AI Overviews and AI Mode.
claims.public_wording: max-snippet caps extraction and normal snippet length; use only with a conscious business decision.
claims.prohibited_wording: max-snippet improves AI Overview visibility without a trade-off.

claims.claim: When Google meta robots rules conflict, the more restrictive rule wins.
claims.public_wording: Conflicting Google meta robots rules resolve to the more restrictive outcome.
claims.prohibited_wording: Meta robots conflicts use the least restrictive outcome.

claims.claim: Google documents redirects and rel=canonical as strong canonical signals and sitemap inclusion as a weak signal.
claims.public_wording: Redirects and rel=canonical are strong signals; sitemap preference is a weaker signal.
claims.prohibited_wording: Sitemap inclusion alone forces Google's canonical.

claims.claim: Google does not recommend using noindex to select a canonical within a site.
claims.public_wording: Use rel=canonical or redirects for duplicate consolidation instead of noindex.
claims.prohibited_wording: Use noindex to pick the canonical.

claims.claim: Non-HTML resources such as PDFs can specify a canonical through the HTTP Link header.
claims.public_wording: Use the HTTP Link header when a non-HTML resource must declare a canonical.
claims.prohibited_wording: A PDF can only be canonicalized through HTML.

claims.claim: Google recommends specifying the canonical in source HTML and avoiding JavaScript that changes an existing canonical.
claims.public_wording: Set one consistent canonical preference and avoid JavaScript rewriting a source-HTML canonical.
claims.prohibited_wording: Set one canonical in HTML and a different one with JavaScript.

claims.claim: Internal links should point to the preferred canonical URL.
claims.public_wording: Internal links should point to preferred canonical URLs.
claims.prohibited_wording: Internal links can safely point to duplicates at scale.

claims.claim: Google recommends that each paginated page have its own canonical URL instead of canonicalizing to page one.
claims.public_wording: Give each paginated page its own canonical and URL.
claims.prohibited_wording: Canonicalize page 2 and deeper pages to page 1.

claims.claim: Google's crawlers discover paginated content through real anchor links and do not generally trigger JavaScript-only user actions.
claims.public_wording: Paginated content should be discoverable through real anchor URLs.
claims.prohibited_wording: A JavaScript-only Load More button is sufficient crawl discovery.

claims.claim: Google no longer uses rel=next and rel=prev as pagination signals.
claims.public_wording: Do not depend on rel=next/prev for Google pagination discovery.
claims.prohibited_wording: Google requires rel=next/prev for pagination.

claims.claim: Google recommends returning a real 404 for empty or nonsensical faceted combinations.
claims.public_wording: Empty facet combinations should return a real 404 under the requested URL.
claims.prohibited_wording: Redirect all empty filters to a generic no-results page.

claims.claim: Google recommends using ampersands as parameter separators for crawlable faceted URLs.
claims.public_wording: Use conventional ampersand-separated query parameters when facets must be crawlable.
claims.prohibited_wording: Any custom separator is equally easy for crawlers to interpret.

claims.claim: Google's crawl-budget guidance targets very large or rapidly changing sites and says most smaller sites do not need crawl-budget optimization.
claims.public_wording: Crawl-budget optimization is usually material only for very large or rapidly changing sites.
claims.prohibited_wording: Every site has a crawl-budget crisis.

claims.claim: Google states that crawling is necessary for eligibility but is not itself a ranking signal.
claims.public_wording: Improved crawl access can improve discovery and freshness, but crawl frequency itself is not a ranking signal.
claims.prohibited_wording: Higher crawl frequency directly improves rankings.

claims.claim: Good Core Web Vitals thresholds are LCP at or below 2.5 seconds, INP at or below 200 milliseconds, and CLS at or below 0.1 at the 75th percentile.
claims.public_wording: Evaluate LCP, INP, and CLS against Google's current 75th-percentile thresholds.
claims.prohibited_wording: FID remains a Core Web Vital in 2026.

claims.claim: LCP can be decomposed into TTFB, resource load delay, resource load duration, and element render delay.
claims.public_wording: Diagnose LCP by subpart before recommending compression or code changes.
claims.prohibited_wording: Compressing the hero image always improves LCP.

claims.claim: The likely LCP image should not be lazy-loaded.
claims.public_wording: Avoid lazy-loading the likely LCP image and make it discoverable in initial HTML.
claims.prohibited_wording: Lazy-load every image, including the hero image.

claims.claim: INP consists of input delay, processing duration, and presentation delay.
claims.public_wording: Diagnose INP by input delay, processing duration, and presentation delay.
claims.prohibited_wording: INP is simply average JavaScript execution time.

claims.claim: Structured data is not documented as a direct ranking factor; it supports eligibility and search-result presentation.
claims.public_wording: Structured data supports eligibility and machine understanding; do not promise ranking gains from markup alone.
claims.prohibited_wording: Adding schema directly raises rankings.

claims.claim: There is no universal word-count target that causes rankings.
claims.public_wording: Content length should follow the task and evidence required, not a universal word-count prescription.
claims.prohibited_wording: A page must contain 2,000 words to rank.

claims.claim: A large observational study associated higher internal-link counts with more Google clicks, but it does not establish a universal 40-to-50-link requirement for every page.
claims.public_wording: Use internal-link graph evidence and page importance rather than a fixed universal link quota.
claims.prohibited_wording: Every important page must have exactly 40 to 50 internal links.

claims.claim: The claim that 97 percent of AI Overview citations come from top-20 pages is not verified in the source corpus.
claims.public_wording: Traditional search visibility and AI citations overlap materially, but the 97 percent figure requires verification.
claims.prohibited_wording: 97 percent of AI Overview citations come from top-20 pages.

claims.claim: The claim that introductions are cited 43 percent more often in AI Overviews is unverified in the source corpus.
claims.public_wording: Answer-first introductions are a useful structural heuristic, but the 43 percent statistic is not approved for external use.
claims.prohibited_wording: Introductions are proven to be cited 43 percent more often.

claims.claim: ITNQ is not confirmed in the corpus as a named, documented Google ranking metric.
claims.public_wording: Design content to satisfy the task and reduce unnecessary follow-up searching; do not present ITNQ as a confirmed ranking metric.
claims.prohibited_wording: Google has confirmed ITNQ as a ranking factor.
